title: Streams, Redirection, Quoting, and Pipelines · Mastery Check
summary: Build a repeatable incident-report pipeline whose data and diagnostics remain distinct and whose failures cannot be hidden by downstream success. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a stream-routing and pipeline-trace workbook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Trace standard input, standard output, standard error, file descriptors, and terminal attachment across a command.
Predict truncating, appending, input, descriptor duplication, and combined redirection from left-to-right order.
Apply unquoted, single-quoted, double-quoted, escaped, and ANSI-C quoted contexts to preserve intended arguments.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Stdin Stdout Stderr evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Stdin Stdout Stderr assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: A 8-record incident command writes selected rows to report.txt and diagnostics to report.err. One grep stage finds no matches; predict descriptor routing and final status with pipefail before running.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Stdin Stdout Stderr has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Stdin Stdout Stderr precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A 8-record incident command writes selected rows to report.txt and diagnostics to report.err. One grep stage finds no matches; predict descriptor routing and final status with pipefail before running.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: All command output travels through one stream.

options.text: Capture user, directory, environment, target state, and authoritative command behavior
Predict shell parsing, expansion, permissions, side effects, exit status, and safety boundary
Run the smallest simulated observation or reversible change and retain exact output
Verify changed state, failure path, rollback, and an accessible operations handoff

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Command Substitution: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A 5-record incident command writes selected rows to report.txt and diagnostics to report.err. One grep stage finds no matches; predict descriptor routing and final status with pipefail before running.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use command substitution and grouping without losing newlines, status, scope, or observable failure evidence.
